target.name: malayamycin A
target.motivation: Malayamycin A is a novel bicyclic perhydrofuropyran C-nucleoside with broad-spectrum fungicidal activity, showing higher potency in planta than in vitro, suggesting potential to overcome resistance to existing fungicides. There is a high demand for new antifungal compounds.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: RCM reaction for pyran ring construction, followed by alkene functionalization to install 1,2-cis-hydroxy amine.

success_criteria.free_text: Rapid access to the core perhydrofuropyran skeleton of malayamycin A and other related uracil nucleosides, enabling future rapid derivatization.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Employing oxazoline to unmask the 1,2-hydroxyamine moiety, eliminating the need for alkene functionalization required in previous endeavors.
To construct the bicyclic perhydrofuropyran core from a nitrone-based precursor 7.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy aims to develop a practical route to the perhydrofuropyran core of malayamycin A using intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition and a latent functionality approach with an oxazoline, circumventing the lengthy alkene functionalization steps of previous syntheses.

thought.background: The starting material diacetone-D-allofuranose (8) is readily available. The goal is to introduce a propargyl group and then prepare a nitrone for intramolecular cycloaddition.
thought.gap: Efficient introduction of the propargyl group and subsequent deprotection to allow for oxidative cleavage.
thought.decision: To introduce the propargyl group, used NaH and propargyl bromide, followed by AcOH for deprotection, expecting to form diol 9.

outcome.observation: Diol 9 was obtained after propargyl group introduction and deprotection.

evidence.text_span: Based on the known protocol [33], diacetone-ᴅ-allofuranose 8 was first introduced with a propargyl group (Scheme 2A). Upon treatment of AcOH to afford diol 9

thought.background: Diol 9 is available. The next step is to prepare the nitrone for intramolecular cycloaddition.
thought.gap: Efficient oxidative cleavage of the diol and subsequent condensation to form the nitrone.
thought.decision: To form the nitrone, used NaIO4 on silica gel for oxidative cleavage to an aldehyde, which was immediately condensed with benzylhydroxylamine, expecting to form nitrone 10.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde was formed and immediately subjected to condensation with benzylhydroxylamine to form nitrone 10.

evidence.text_span: oxidative cleavage with Shing’s protocol $\mathrm { N a I O _ { 4 } }$ on silica gel) [34] proceeded smoothly to deliver the aldehyde which was immediately subjected to the condensation reaction with benzylhydroxylamine. The corresponding nitrone 10 then underwent an intramolecular cycloaddition.

thought.background: Nitrone 10 is formed. The next step is the intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition.
thought.gap: Achieving the desired stereochemistry at C3 during the cycloaddition.
thought.decision: To form the cycloadduct, allowed nitrone 10 to undergo intramolecular cycloaddition, expecting adduct 11.

outcome.observation: Adduct 11 was isolated as the major product in 42% yield for 2 steps (from 9). Comprehensive NMR analysis revealed undesired stereochemistry at C3 due to a possible chair-like transition state.

evidence.text_span: The corresponding nitrone 10 then underwent an intramolecular cycloaddition. Adduct 11 was isolated as the major product in $4 2 \%$ yield for 2 steps. Comprehensive NMR analysis revealed the undesired stereochemistry at C3 due to a possible chair-like transition state like 10a (Scheme 2B).

thought.background: Adduct 11 has undesired stereochemistry at C3. It was anticipated that late-stage epimerization might invert the configuration. The next step is dihydroxylation.
thought.gap: Converting the alkene to a diol for further oxidative cleavage.
thought.decision: To form the diol, subjected alkene 11 to dihydroxylation conditions, expecting diol 12.

outcome.observation: Diol 12 was obtained as a mixture of inseparable isomers.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, dihydroxylation [37] readily converted alkene 11 to diol 12 as a mixture of inseparable isomers.

thought.background: Diol 12 was obtained as a mixture of inseparable isomers. The next step is oxidative cleavage.
thought.gap: Achieving the desired product after oxidative cleavage without fragmentation.
thought.decision: To cleave the diol, subjected diol 12 to oxidative cleavage with NaIO4, expecting a different product than what was observed.

outcome.observation: Oxidative cleavage with NaIO4 resulted in a compound with strong UV absorption, identified as enone 14. It is assumed that the formyloxy group on the N atom in unstable intermediate 13 facilitated fragmentation.

evidence.text_span: Without purification, oxidative cleavage with $\mathrm { N a I O _ { 4 } }$ resulted in a compound with strong UV absorption, which was eventually identified as enone 14 (Scheme 3). It is assumed that the formyloxy group on the N atom in the unstable intermediate 13 serves as electron-withdrawing group to facilitate fragmentation when removal of the acidic proton at C2 was initiated.

thought.background: The previous attempt to cleave the diol 12 resulted in enone 14 due to fragmentation. The electron-withdrawing group on the nitrogen atom is suspected to be the cause.
thought.gap: Cleaving the N-O bond prior to breaking the C2-C2' bond to avoid fragmentation.
thought.decision: Attempted several conditions to cleave the N-O bond in 11 or 12, expecting to prevent fragmentation.

outcome.observation: Several conditions to cleave the N–O bond in 11 or 12 had not yielded any successful outcome.

evidence.text_span: However, several conditions to cleave the N–O bond in 11 or 12 had not yielded any successful outcome.

thought.background: Previous attempts to form the desired bicyclic core resulted in undesired stereochemistry and fragmentation. The electron-withdrawing group on the nitrogen atom was problematic.
thought.gap: A modified synthetic sequence is needed to switch the oxidation states at C2 and C3 and avoid the issues encountered.
thought.decision: To circumvent the influence of the electron-withdrawing group on the nitrogen atom, decided to adjust the synthetic sequence to switch the oxidation states at C2 and C3, starting with diacetone-D-allofuranose (8) and crotyl bromide (15).

outcome.observation: A modified route was pursued to access the core of malayamycins.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, we turned to adjust the synthetic sequence to switch the oxidation states at C2 and C3 (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Starting with diacetone-D-allofuranose (8), the goal is to introduce a crotyl group and then deprotect the acetonide.
thought.gap: Efficiently introducing the crotyl group and hydrolyzing the acetonide.
thought.decision: To introduce the crotyl group, used NaH and crotyl bromide (15), followed by 70% AcOH for hydrolysis of the acetonide group, expecting to form diol 16.

outcome.observation: Compound 16 was obtained as a stereoisomeric mixture of olefin, resulting from the use of crotyl bromide as a mixture of geometric isomers.

evidence.text_span: It should be noted that compound 16 [38] was obtained as a stereoisomeric mixture of olefin, resulting from the use of crotyl bromide as a mixture of geometric isomers. After installation of the crotyl group, hydrolysis of the acetonide group

thought.background: Diol 16 is available as a stereoisomeric mixture. The next step is oxidative cleavage and oxime formation.
thought.gap: Efficiently performing oxidative cleavage and condensation to form the oxime.
thought.decision: To form the oxime, subjected diol 16 to oxidative cleavage with NaIO4/SiO2, followed by condensation with hydroxylamine, expecting oxime 17.

outcome.observation: Oxime 17 was prepared in overall 59% yield (from 16).

evidence.text_span: and oxidative cleavage of diol 16, oxime 17 was prepared through the condensation of the aldehyde with hydroxylamine in overall $5 9 \%$ yield.

thought.background: Oxime 17 is available. The next step is intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition.
thought.gap: Efficiently generating the nitrile N-oxide in situ and performing the cycloaddition.
thought.decision: To form the cycloadducts, oxidized oxime 17 with NaClO·5H2O to generate the nitrile N-oxide in situ, which immediately underwent intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition, expecting cycloadducts 18.

outcome.observation: Cycloadducts 18 were delivered in good yield as a mixture of two inseparable diastereoisomers (dr 4:1). This telescoped step was readily performed on a gram scale without interrupted purification of the nitrile oxide.

evidence.text_span: Upon oxidation with $\mathrm { N a C l O } { \cdot } 5 \mathrm { H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O }$ , the in situ-generated nitrile $N .$ -oxide immediately underwent intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition to deliver the cycloadducts 18 in good yield as a mixture of two inseparable diastereoisomers. This telescoped step was readily performed on a gram scale without interrupted purification of the nitrile oxide.

thought.background: Cycloadducts 18 are available as a mixture of diastereoisomers. The next step is reduction of the imine and Boc protection.
thought.gap: Efficiently reducing the imine and protecting the resulting amine, while considering the acid-sensitive acetonide group.
thought.decision: To reduce the imine and protect the amine, subjected the mixture of 18 to reduction with NaBH3CN in AcOH–MeOH, and immediately protected with Boc2O and DMAP, expecting Boc-protected product 19.

outcome.observation: The isolated yield of 19 was moderate because the anomeric carbon at C7 is associated with an acid-sensitive acetonide group. The major product 19a was confirmed by NMR and X-ray analysis.

evidence.text_span: The mixture of 18 was subjected to reduction of the imine motif by $\mathrm { N a B H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C N }$ in AcOH–MeOH and immediately protected with the Boc group. The isolated yield of 19 was moderate because the anomeric carbon at C7 is associated with an acid-sensitive acetonide group. The structural determination of the major product 19a was accomplished by comprehensive NMR spectroscopy and further unambiguously confirmed by X-ray analysis (see Supporting Information File 1 for details).

thought.background: Boc-protected product 19 (mixture of 19a and 19b) is available. The next steps are reductive cleavage of the N-O bond and oxidation of the secondary alcohol.
thought.gap: Efficiently cleaving the N-O bond and oxidizing the alcohol to a ketone.
thought.decision: To cleave the N-O bond, used Mo(CO)6 in refluxing CH3CN. Subsequent oxidation of the resulting secondary alcohol was performed with Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP), expecting methyl ketone 20.

outcome.observation: Methyl ketone 20 was afforded in 53% yield for 2 steps. The minor isomer 19b also yielded a product identical to 20, indicating that both stereoisomers from the INOC cycloaddition convert to a single stereoisomer of 20.

evidence.text_span: The following reductive cleavage of the N–O bond was carried out by $\mathrm { Mo(CO) _ { 6 } }$ in refluxing $\mathrm { C H } _ { 3 } \mathrm { C N }$ [39]. Subsequent oxidation of the resulting secondary alcohol with Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP) [40] afforded methyl ketone 20 in $5 3 \%$ yield for 2 steps. Moreover, the minor isomer 19b also underwent the above two-step sequence, yielding a product identical to 20 (the synthetic route is not shown). This indicates that the pair of diastereomers essentially differs only at the C2’ position arised from the isomeric mixture of crotyl bromide 15. It is also worth noting that both stereoisomers derived from the INOC cycloaddition can be converted to a single stereoisomer of 20.

thought.background: Methyl ketone 20 has all required stereogenic centers embedded. The remaining problem is converting C to O to install the secondary alcohol at C2 with retention of the β-configuration via Baeyer–Villiger (BV) oxidation.
thought.gap: Achieving the Baeyer–Villiger oxidation to form the desired acetate 21.
thought.decision: Attempted Baeyer–Villiger oxidation on ketone 20 using several oxidants, expecting acetate 21.

outcome.observation: It turns out very challenging for the BV oxidation. Several oxidants were examined and the desired acetate 21 remains inaccessible. Steric hindrance of the Boc protecting group might have a great impact on reactivity.

evidence.text_span: The programmed Baeyer–Villiger (BV) oxidation would be a feasible transformation to furnish all necessary functional groups for the completion of the core skeleton in malayamycins. To our surprise, it turns out very challenging for the BV oxidation. Several oxidants were examined and the desired acetate 21 remains inaccessible (Scheme 5) [41]. We assumed the steric hinderance of the Boc protecting group might have a great impact to the reactivity of ketone. Investigation along this line is currently on the way.

validation.significance: The streamlined dipolar cycloaddition approach provides rapid access to the core perhydrofuropyran skeleton of malayamycin A, circumventing lengthy alkene functionalization. This strategy offers a reliable platform for accessing diverse uracil nucleosides and derivatives, facilitating the development of potent fungicides, despite remaining challenges in the final steps.

source.title: Rapid access to the core of malayamycin A by intramolecular dipolar cycloaddition.
source.authors: Liu Y, Yang Y, Yang C, Huang SH, Jin J, Hong R.
